topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a stark reminder of the hours I’d poured into that wretched piece of writing. It was my personal statement, the culmination of months spent agonizing over how to frame my supposed 'unique perspective.' Clicking it opened the file, and there it was: my essay, perfectly formatted, sitting beside a gleaming, anonymized example titled, "The Quiet Observer." My jaw tightened. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. I reread the prose, the carefully chosen metaphors, and felt a slow, burning heat rise in my chest. It was polished, effortless, and utterly hollow. I slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet apartment. I didn't bother to delete the file; I just stared at the screen, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my sternum.

[story 2]

I picked up the printed copy from the admissions office. The paper felt crisp, the font immaculate. My eyes scanned the paragraphs, and a distinct sense of profound irritation washed over me. This was someone else’s success story, neatly packaged for consumption. I tapped a finger against a sentence about 'navigating ambiguity,' a phrase I had labored over for weeks, only to see it deployed so casually here. A dry, humorless laugh escaped my lips. I folded the paper with unnecessary force, the creases sharp and unforgiving. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent city traffic, feeling a distinct urge to crumple the paper into a tight ball and toss it into the bin. The sheer presumption of it—that this carefully crafted narrative could be so easily replicated—made my stomach clench with a sour, bitter feeling.

[story 3]

"Look at this," Mark said, sliding his tablet across the worn wooden desk. The screen displayed my application essay, juxtaposed with a sample submission from a highly selective institution. I didn't look up immediately. I just stared at the screen, my gaze lingering on the flawless structure, the eloquent phrasing that seemed to glide effortlessly across the page. When I finally did look up, my expression was flat, bordering on hostile. I let out a slow, controlled breath, the air tasting stale. "It’s… efficient," I managed, my voice low and utterly devoid of inflection. I picked up the printed version, turning it over in my hands as if examining a flawed artifact. The ease with which they had achieved this level of articulation struck me as profoundly insulting. I placed the document back down, the movement deliberate and stiff, a clear signal that I had no intention of engaging with this manufactured perfection.
